learn.title: Solar Panel Types Compared
learn.intro: Understanding solar panel types is crucial for optimizing renewable energy adoption. Monocrystalline, polycrystalline, and thin-film panels each offer unique trade-offs in efficiency, cost, and application. Choosing the right technology accelerates clean energy deployment while balancing economic and environmental priorities in the global energy transition.
learn.c1_text: Made from single-crystal silicon, these panels achieve 20-22% efficiency. Think of them as high-performance athletes—costlier but powerful. They perform well in low light and have a long lifespan. Ideal for rooftops with limited space.
learn.c2_text: Constructed from melted silicon fragments, these panels offer 15-20% efficiency. Like a budget-friendly sedan, they’re affordable but less efficient. Best for large installations where space isn’t constrained and upfront costs matter most.
learn.c3_text: Using materials like cadmium telluride, these flexible panels reach 10-12% efficiency. Imagine solar paint—lightweight and versatile. India’s Chennai Metro uses thin-film for its 2 MW rooftop project, showcasing urban adaptability.
learn.stat3: Thin-film requires 50% less material than crystalline panels

learn.title: Floating Solar Farms Explained
learn.intro: Floating solar farms transform water bodies into clean energy hubs while conserving water. By reducing evaporation and land use, they address dual challenges of energy and resource scarcity. Their innovative design supports sustainable growth in densely populated regions and arid climates.
learn.c1_text: Covering 1% of a reservoir reduces evaporation by 60%, saving 1.5 million liters annually. Like a parasol over a pool, floating panels block sunlight. China’s 150 MW Huainan plant prevents 3.1 million m³ of water loss yearly.
learn.c2_text: Water-cooled panels operate 10-15°C cooler, boosting efficiency by 5-10%. Think of a car radiator—cooling improves performance. Floating arrays also reduce algae growth, cutting water treatment costs.
learn.c3_text: India’s 10 MW Kerala floating plant powers 7,000 homes. The 500 MW target by 2025 could save 200 million liters of water daily. Policies like the Solar Pump Irrigators’ Cooperative model promote dual-use water-energy systems.
learn.stat1: Floating solar reduces water evaporation by 60%
learn.stat2: India aims for 500 MW floating solar by 2025
learn.stat4: 1 GW floating solar saves 300 million liters of water annually

learn.title: Pumped Hydro Storage Basics
learn.intro: Pumped hydro storage (PHS) underpins grid stability by storing excess energy as gravitational potential. It’s the world’s largest battery, accounting for 94% of global energy storage. PHS enables renewable integration while providing critical backup for peak demand and emergencies.
learn.c1_text: During surplus, water is pumped uphill to a reservoir. When demand rises, it flows down through turbines to generate electricity. Imagine a giant water battery—storing energy as elevated water, releasing it on demand. Efficiency ranges from 70-85%.
learn.c2_text: The 26 GW U.S. Bath County PHS in Virginia holds 24,000 MWh. China’s 32 GW Fengning plant powers 1 million homes. PHS accounts for 95% of global storage capacity, dwarfing lithium-ion systems by 10x.
learn.c3_text: India’s 100 MW Kundah PHS in Karnataka stores 400 MWh. The 2,000 MW target by 2030 aims to stabilize grids with 25 GW solar-wind hybrid projects. PHS complements India’s renewable expansion with reliable baseload storage.
learn.stat1: PHS stores 95% of global energy storage capacity
learn.stat2: India’s 100 MW Kundah PHS operational since 2022

learn.title: Hydrogen Color Codes Explained
learn.intro: Hydrogen’s ‘color’ denotes its production method and carbon footprint. Grey, blue, and green hydrogen represent varying sustainability levels. Understanding these distinctions is vital for decarbonizing industries and transportation, where direct electrification isn’t feasible. The shift to green hydrogen is pivotal for net-zero goals.
learn.c1_text: Produced via steam methane reforming without carbon capture, grey hydrogen emits 10 kg CO₂/kg H₂. Think of it as coal-fired electricity—common but polluting. It accounts for 95% of global hydrogen production today.
learn.c2_text: Same as grey but with 90% CO₂ captured and stored. Like a hybrid car—cleaner but still fossil-dependent. Blue hydrogen reduces emissions by 50-70% compared to grey, serving as a transitional solution.
learn.c3_text: Electrolysis using renewable energy, producing zero emissions. Imagine a leaf splitting water—clean and natural. India’s 10 MW Gujarat electrolyzer aims to produce 12,000 kg/day, supporting its $2.3/kg green hydrogen target by 2030.
learn.stat1: Grey hydrogen emits 10 kg CO₂/kg H₂

learn.title: Green Hydrogen Production Methods
learn.intro: Green hydrogen production via water electrolysis is critical for decarbonizing heavy industries and transport. Proton exchange membrane (PEM) and alkaline electrolyzers offer distinct advantages in efficiency, cost, and scalability. Choosing the right technology accelerates India’s hydrogen economy goals.
learn.c1_text: PEM uses a solid polymer membrane, achieving 60-70% efficiency. Like a fuel cell in reverse, it responds quickly to variable renewable input. Ideal for pairing with solar/wind farms. Costs $1,000-1,500/kW, dropping rapidly with scale.
learn.c2_text: Using liquid electrolyte, alkaline systems reach 60-65% efficiency. Think of a traditional battery—mature technology with lower costs ($500-1,000/kW). Better for steady, large-scale production. India’s 10 MW Gujarat plant uses alkaline tech.
learn.c3_text: India’s 10 MW Gujarat electrolyzer produces 12,000 kg/day. The National Hydrogen Mission targets 5 MMT/year by 2030. Policies incentivize 10 GW electrolyzer capacity, leveraging solar-wind hybrid projects for green hydrogen hubs.
learn.stat4: India targets 5 MMT green hydrogen/year by 2030

learn.title: Offshore Wind Farms Overview
learn.intro: Offshore wind farms harness stronger, more consistent winds to generate massive clean energy. Their design and grid integration require advanced engineering to overcome marine challenges. As India targets 30 GW offshore wind by 2030, understanding these systems is key to unlocking coastal energy potential.
learn.c1_text: Offshore turbines reach 15 MW with 160m rotor diameters. Think of them as skyscrapers at sea—larger blades capture more wind. Fixed foundations dominate shallow waters, while floating platforms unlock 80% of global offshore potential.
learn.c2_text: High-voltage direct current (HVDC) cables transmit power 500 km onshore. Like a highway for electrons, HVDC minimizes losses. Substations stabilize voltage. The UK’s 3.6 GW Hornsea Project connects via HVDC to power 3 million homes.
learn.c3_text: India’s first 1 GW Gujarat offshore wind project begins in 2024. The 30 GW target by 2030 aims to power 20 million homes. Policies streamline auctions and port infrastructure. Floating wind pilot projects target 100 MW by 2025.
learn.stat2: India’s 1 GW Gujarat offshore project under development
learn.stat3: Offshore turbines reach 15 MW with 160m rotors
learn.stat4: India targets 30 GW offshore wind by 2030
